title: Elderflower infusion for colds and congestion
subtitle: A diaphoretic infusion to ease the first days of a cold
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried elderflowers, taken at the onset of a cold or mild flu. Traditionally used to promote perspiration and ease the early feverish, blocked stage of an upper respiratory infection.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, elderflower as a diaphoretic for colds and influenza. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), elder flower preparations for catarrh. EMA HMPC monograph on Sambuci flos (2018), confirms traditional use for relief of early cold symptoms.

content.text: A cup of elderflower tea at the very first sign of a cold. The stage when the throat starts to prickle, the nose feels blocked, and the body is just starting to warm up. Elderflower is the tradition's classic

for that early stage. It nudges gentle sweating and eases chest congestion. Drunk hot in bed with the window closed, it is one of the oldest home comfort measures in the western kitchen. Old herbals list elder flowers as a first-call herb for the early days of a cold. The brew is as simple as a cup of tea.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Elderflower is well tolerated at tea strength. It is not the same as elderberries, which must be cooked before use.

Pregnancy: a cup of elderflower tea at tea strength is generally fine. Talk to your midwife before stronger doses or daily use.

See a doctor for a high fever that does not start to ease in a couple of days, for any fever in a young baby, for trouble breathing or chest pain, for a stiff neck with headache and fever, or for cold symptoms when the immune system is suppressed.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried elderflowers
items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon of dried flowers

content.text: Place {{dried-elderflowers}} of dried elderflowers in a teapot or infuser. Boil the kettle, wait 30 seconds, then pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over the flowers. Lid the pot immediately. Steep for 10 minutes, the lid keeps the volatile oils in the cup. Strain through the infuser or a fine sieve. Drink hot, while the cup is still steaming, and ideally rest in bed afterwards with extra blankets.

One cup at the onset of symptoms. Up to three cups a day for the first two to three days of a cold, morning, afternoon, and evening, for adults. Drink hot and rest; the combination of warmth, rest, and fluid is at least as important as the herb itself. If symptoms worsen, persist beyond five days, or include a high fever, see your doctor.

Dried elderflowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin or glass jar away from light and heat. Flowers that have lost their delicate honey-like scent when the tin is opened are past their best. Brew each cup fresh, elderflower tea does not store.
When NOT to use this remedy

See a doctor for a high fever that does not start to ease in a couple of days. See a doctor for any fever in a baby. Seek care for trouble breathing or chest pain. Seek care for a stiff neck with headache and fever. Seek care for cold or flu symptoms when the immune system is suppressed, or for anyone on drugs that lower the immune response.
